title: Fun with Graph States: Nonlocal Bell Pairs and the Arf Invariant
authors: Bartlomiej Czech, Yichen Feng, Xianlai Wu, Minjun Xie
abstract: We study inner products and partial amplitudes of graph states--a commonly employed class of quantum states, which are specified by graphs. We find that the magnitudes of these quantities are simply related to the rank of the adjacency matrix of the graph over F_2 while the phase is determined by the Arf invariant of its quadratic refinement. These facts motivate a nonlocal tensor factorization of the Hilbert space, with respect to which all graph states are products of Bell pairs with unentangled ancillae. These results may illuminate the quantum advantage in the framework of Measurement-Based Quantum Computation and suggest that graph states can be usefully visualized in the language of algebraic topology. In addition, we develop a specialized technique for computing expectation values of qubit-wise permutations in graph states, which is useful for calculating multi-invariants.
summary: This work rigorously analyzes graph states by connecting their inner products and amplitudes to fundamental invariants from algebraic topology, specifically the Arf invariant. It establishes a nonlocal tensor factorization basis for these states, suggesting that quantum advantage in MBQC can be understood through topological structures on auxiliary manifolds.
main_problem: The study investigates the inner product $\langle G'|G''angle$ between graph states, relating its magnitude to algebraic invariants like the rank of the adjacency matrix over $\mathbb{F}_2$, and its phase to the Arf invariant.
main_result: Graph states can be factorized into a nonlocal tensor basis composed of Bell pairs and unentangled ancillae, providing deep connections between quantum information theory and algebraic topology.
method: The analysis employs techniques from linear algebra over $\mathbb{F}_2$, utilizing the structure of stabilizer codes defined by graph adjacency matrices to compute partial amplitudes and expectation values.
model_or_system: The system is based on graph states $|Gangle$ defined on $M$ qubits, which are a specific class of stabilizer states generated by CZ gates determined by the graph's adjacency matrix $A$.
key_observables: Inner product $\langle G'|G''angle$, magnitude $|\langle+|^{\otimes M} |Gangle|$, phase factor related to the Arf invariant ($\Delta$), and multi-invariants like Rényi entropy.
important_parameters: The rank of the adjacency matrix $A$ over $\mathbb{F}_2$, and the Arf invariant $\Delta$.
main_assumptions: Calculations are performed rigorously within the framework of Pauli algebra on qubits, often assuming non-vanishing amplitudes to define topological invariants.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the factorization of graph states into effective Bell pairs and unentangled ancillae; other figures show structures related to counting solutions for multi-invariants (e.g., magic squares/annuli).
paper_structure: The paper progresses from defining the inner product $\langle G'|G''angle$ using linear algebra over $\mathbb{F}_2$, establishing that magnitude depends on rank and phase on the Arf invariant. It then develops a nonlocal tensor factorization, applies this to compute multi-invariants (like Rényi entropy), and finally maps these results onto topological invariants of auxiliary manifolds.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful mathematical framework linking quantum resource states (graph states) directly to deep concepts in algebraic topology (Arf invariant, intersection forms), which is highly relevant for understanding the fundamental limits of computation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Figure 4: The complete graph K4 as a union of graph G (red) and π(G) (blue), where π = (1 2 3 4) permutes the vertices of G. We have drawn the figure such that π can be visualized as an anti-clockwise rotation by 90◦.
Figure 5: A k-replica multi-invariant of a q-partite quantum state |G′⟩A1A2...Aq involves a choice of q permutations πi, each acting on the k replicas of party Ai.
Figure 6: Stabilizers O⃗x, which contribute to Z(3) n in summation (5.17) are in one-to-one correspondence with magic squares—solutions of equations (B.6-B.7).
Figure 7: Stabilizers O⃗x, which contribute to Z2n in summation (5.17) are in one-to-one correspondence with “magic annuli”—solutions of equations (B.13-B.15).
